title: One Pleasure Before Bed
subtitle: A small evening activity for softening the nervous system through deliberate enjoyment
excerpt: A simple activity from Day 24 of a 30-day sleep program for practising pleasure as a pre-sleep tool. One small enjoyable act, done on purpose and without guilt: a specific drink, a few pages of a book, five minutes outside. The act signals the nervous system that the day is over and rest is next.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education, developed for the activity theme of Day 24 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use as a regular evening transition — a signal to the body that the productive part of the day is done and rest is next.

content.text: A short evening activity for using deliberate pleasure as a bridge between the active day and sleep. The practice is not about winding down in the conventional sense (lists, screens off, strict routines). It is about adding one small act of real enjoyment that tells the body the pressure is over.

Choose one small pleasurable act to do in the last hour before bed. It should be something that actually appeals — not something that seems like it should help, but something you would enjoy without reservation if time and guilt were not factors.
A cup of something you enjoy slowly, sitting down without doing anything else.
Ten minutes outside in whatever weather is there — the cold or the dark counts.
A few pages of a book you are reading for pleasure, not improvement.
A piece of something good to eat, taken without speed.
Ten minutes of music with nothing else happening.
The point is the deliberateness. Doing it on purpose, knowing it counts as part of how you prepare for rest, rather than as a guilty gap before you do the proper work of going to sleep.
Do it this week, every evening, even briefly. The regularity builds the signal. After a few nights the body begins to recognise it.
Original to homemade.education, developed for the activity theme of Day 24 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
